findings.headline: 61% of probable dropouts concentrate in the top 10% of schools.
findings.body: Of 6,536 historic dropouts, ~3,973 came from just 912 schools. Infrastructure and intervention budget should follow this concentration.

findings.headline: Recent attendance deterioration is a sharper signal than average attendance.
findings.body: Dropouts show +3.2 pp more late-year attendance decline than non-dropouts, while their overall attendance gap is 18.8 pp. Decline velocity > absolute rate.

findings.headline: A 'silent decline' cohort hides behind decent marks.
findings.body: Among students with >50% cohort-normalized marks but >15pp recent-attendance decline, dropout rate is 2.00% — well above the base rate. They won't be caught by marks-only early warning.

findings.headline: ~3,825 students sit in a high-risk / high-recoverability segment.
findings.body: These students show attendance instability but retain enough academic momentum to respond to targeted intervention. Highest return per counsellor-hour.

findings.headline: First-60-day attendance alone captures 61% of eventual dropouts in the top-10% risk band.
findings.body: A lightweight model using only demographics + first 30-60 days of attendance reproduces a large share of the full-year champion's capture. Enables HYPER-EARLY detection — intervention by Aug-Sep.

findings.headline: Certain districts skew towards structural absenteeism vs academic decline.
findings.body: Districts vary in which driver dominates. Districts with chronic-absence dominance need home-visit / transport / migration-verification capacity; districts with academic-decline dominance need remedial teacher deployment. Intervention mix should follow driver mix, not default to uniform.
